speaker: interviewer
text: --in Auschwitz. It's 1944. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you-- getting back into the life of Auschwitz, you know, that-- the daily hardship of getting enough food. And as I remember, we used to get coffee there which wasn't fit to drink. And we used to-- I used to brush my teeth with that coffee, you know, because it was something warm. And we used to get a slice of-- slice of salami and a slice of bread and a bowl of soup, you know, which if the-- if the man that was dishing up the soup liked you, he would scoop it down from the bottom. And you'd have something, you know, to eat. If he didn't-- if he didn't know you, he would give you from the top. It would be mostly water.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a daily struggle to-- getting enough food. We used to sneak behind the kitchen to get some potato peels. So we could eat the potato peels just for sustenance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and then, periodic-- another thing that happened to me in Auschwitz that-- I, I had a toothache a couple times. And I had some bad teeth. And, of course, they didn't bother fixing them. They just yanked them out. You know, that's how I lost some of my teeth. Because once you start losing teeth, it affects the other teeth. And my teeth got progressively worse and worse. Another time, I remember that I was eating some bread. And it must have been spoiled bread because I got deathly sick. And my temperature shot up-- way up. And I was feverish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took me to the hospital. And they pumped-- they shoved the tube down my throat. And they put some suction on it. And they emptied my stomach. And, and that's what saved my life, you know, from the poisoned food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and then there was the daily struggle. You know, every once in a while, they grabbed you to unload railroad cars with cement bags. And they were quite heavy. They were like, 60-pound, 70-pound bags. In the weakened condition, you know, it was quite rough to, to carry it. And that's how our situation was. A lot-- some people tried to escape. And they got caught. And they got hung. They, they gathered us on the-- on the Umschlagplatz. And we had to watch the hanging of those people. And I remember one man, he was kicking. You know, he didn't die fast enough. So one of the Germans pulled on his leg-- on his legs, yanked on him, to make him die quicker. And also, this standing on Umschlagplatz every day, which was like torture, you know. They kept you for hours while they counted-- back and forth and back and forth-- to make sure that everybody was present. And if somebody wasn't present, if they were-- thought they were missing somebody, you stood another hour or two. To make sure that they find them, they sent out a search party to look for the people. And so, like I said, this was a very hard part of it. And I-- also walking to work in those wooden [GERMAN]-- wooden shoes was, like, torturous, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk to your friends, your comrades at night--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --about what would happen during the day? Do you remember any of those conversations? What did you talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what we were-- mainly, what we were talking about is about our families, where, where, where we're from and where-- what were your-- what was your father doing and your mother. And just, you know, you had to talk to somebody, you know, this-- and basically, that's what I remember. And I remember that there was a sort of dislike, unfortunately, between one group of Jews and the other one. Like the Hungarians Jews didn't like the Polish Jews. And the Polish Jews didn't like Hungarian Jews. Because I guess it was the language barrier and the-- and their way of living and different-- difference. And-- but otherwise, there was nothing much really entertaining or anything in this life, you know. And basically, that went on until, like I said, that the Russians were getting closer. And they evacuated us. Like I mentioned previously, they give us that ration of bread, which I hung around my neck. And that was in the death of winter. It was very cold, freezing. And then we had to walk in the snow. And the stragglers, you know, people that couldn't keep up, that couldn't walk, weak-- or women, you know, that were hanging back, we heard shots continuously. They were shot, you know. They couldn't keep up as the group. And at, at night, we were huddled together to try and keep warm. And then one morning I got up, and my ration of bread was gone. Apparently, somebody must have snipped off the string from-- while I was asleep. And here I was faced with not having anything to eat at all. But I was working together with a young boy-- young guy-- young man from Slovakia. He was a Slovak. And when I told him about my problem, he gave me half of his ration bread. And he wasn't Jewish even. But he shared with me his ration bread. Maybe that's what saved my life, too. And then after we got-- I think it was Breslau, you know, when we got to-- and who was-- who, who was alive-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where they packed us into open railroad cars. It was in winter. But they were completely open. And it was snowing on us. And we were huddling together, trying to, you know, keep from freezing. And they drove us to Buchenwald. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then in Buchenwald-- when we arrived at Buchenwald, they sent us to-- there were, like, enormous barracks, huge barracks. And when you walked in, there was like a huge hangar almost-- size, you know. And on each side, there were rows of those wooden pallets stacked up maybe six or eight high, you know, like you put merchandise in. And there was no straw or anything, you know, just on it. And they-- we were packed like six to a pallet. So you could only lay on one side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you could lay a few hours on one side. Then we had to all together shift over to lay on the other side. Till you got sores on your bones from laying there, you know. And basically, there was no work there, you know. It was just warehousing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- at one time or another, I don't remember the place. While we were in Buchenwald, they shipped us to another area of Germany, which was like in a mountainous area-- pardon me-- where they had factories inside mountains, like a dug-- excavate into the side of mountains, where they had the railroad coming in there. And they, they had machine shops. And I remember I was working on a machine there, on a lathe, making parts. I don't remember what they were anymore, but something for the defense, I guess. And they kept us there for a while. And then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, I would say probably about three or four months-- three months or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, there was a beautiful area, you know. It was like in mountainous-- like you see a picture card, 'Currier Ives' type of winter picture card with mountains and, and, and trees, you know, Christmas trees and everything, you know. And we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Germany, but it was a mountainous area. I don't remember, you know. Nobody told us what it was or where. You know, you were just a piece of machinery to be moved around, you know. Nobody told you, here, we're going here. Or here, we're going there, you know. But it wasn't that bad over there because we did work inside-- indoors. And, and, and we-- the place where we slept was a lot better, nicer. Unfortunately, that didn't last. They, they took us back to Buchenwald in the railroad cars. And when we arrived in Buchenwald, it was already towards the end of the war. Because we could hear every day artillery, you know, coming closer and closer-- and that everybody's saying, oh, the Russians are coming closer. The Russians are coming close. We didn't know who it was, whether it was Russians or Americans. But we did hear the artillery. Actually, I think it was the Americans. But when the, the war was coming closer to Buchenwald, they started marching people out. They used to gather people and tell them, oh-- with a loudspeaker, they would announce, we're going to take you to a better camp where you get better food, better living conditions. And whoever volunteered, they would take them and march them out into the forest. And they would shoot them there. They used to shoot the people just to get, get rid of witnesses, you know. And we could hear the shots. And the people came back-- some people came back and said, they were all shot. And they were doing that all-- continuously, every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: So myself and some of the people I was with, I think it was three or four of us, we hid under the foundations of the barracks, which was half mud and half dry. You know, it was mostly muddy. And we laid there for, like, four days without any food whatsoever just so they wouldn't take us and march us out. Because we had a sneaking suspicion that if they do, we'll never be alive again, you know. So we were there, like, for four days until one day, it became very quiet, you know. And then we peeked out. And the Germans were gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, the tanks, you know, just crashed through the gates-- American tanks. They were the American soldiers. And we were so emaciated, you know, we couldn't even stand up. You know, we were just laying down. But we were so happy that finally, we were liberated. The curious part about it is that my brother who lived in America was amongst those soldiers who liberated Buchenwald. But he didn't know I was there. And, of course, I didn't know he was there, either. But that's what I found out when I came to this country and I talked to him. And so when they liberated us, of course, they gave us food. And we got very sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought us SPAM and bread and good soups and, you know, real good army food. And we got so sick, you know, dysentery, you know, because the stomachs were not used this rich food. And shortly after that, I got what they call in German Fleckfieber, which is like a typhus. And they-- so they took me to a hospital in Weimar. There was a town named Weimar, in Thuringia, which is near Buchenwald. And I was in that hospital for a few weeks. And I was-- I had such a high fever-- what they used to do, they used to soak sheets in ice-cold water and wrap me up in those sheets, off and on, off and on, to bring the fever down. And then finally they did bring it down, you know. So I recovered from that. And after that, they sent me as a-- for convalescence, they sent me to a-- they, they took some German homes, which they used to place convalescing survivors. And I was in one of those homes to convalesce. And, and that's how I survived. And after a while, we started walking the streets of Weimar. And the Germans had it quite well. I mean, even if they lost the war, you know, they had plenty to eat and everything, you know. They had restaurants open and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were walking around. And that's how I met my wife first. I saw her standing in front of one of the houses. She was living with a girlfriend. And that's how we met. And we just met one time, once or twice. And then I didn't see her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Incidentally, what happened-- the Germans took over Weimar, Thuringia. They took over so this whole section of Germany. America gave up the section of Germany to them-- to the Russians. And when the Russians came in, all of a sudden, we didn't have any rations because they took the food for themselves-- all the supplies. So it became tough. But the Russian soldiers had plenty money, because they, they requisitioned the printing, printing presses that printed the marks. So they had whole sheets of marks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I became friendly with some Russian soldiers. They were quite nice, nice guys. And because I spoke Polish, which is similar to Russian, and I picked up some Russian from them, and I spoke German, I, I could-- I was, like, an intermediary between them and the-- and the German-- especially the German girls that they wanted to meet, you know. So I used to introduce them to the German girls, and so on. So they gave us some marks, you know. So that made it a little easier. We had some marks to spend.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans-- to the German, a mark was a lot of money, when the Russians had whole sheets of them. They were not even cut, you know. So that comes to my mind. And-- but after a while-- I was with a few friends. And we decided to get out of the Russian side. Because we felt if we were going to be on the Russian side, sooner or later, they'll send us back to Russia. They did send a lot of Polish people-- Polish Jews back to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we wanted to get out in the worst way. So one time, we, we got on a train that was going from the Russian, Russian Zone to the American Zone. But they caught us. So-- Who caught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians because they-- you know, they-- when the Russian train stopped at the border, they asked to see your papers. And when they saw that we were Polish, you know, Jews, you know-- Jews from Poland, they didn't let us across. They didn't want anybody to get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the first time they caught us, they just gave us a lecture. So they said, if you try again, you go to jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we, we waited a few days. And then we got back on. And we tried to sneak in in a warehouse railroad car, you know, a railroad car that was used for, for shipping merchandise and stuff. But they caught us there, too. And this time, they put us in jail for 24 hours. They said, next time we catch you, you go to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what to do, you know? So-- but we did have some marks with us because of those Russian soldiers. So we bought our way.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, there was a group of Germans repatriating returning soldiers who were imprisoned in Russia and were released in Russia. And they wanted to get back on the other side to their homeland-- from the Russian Zone to it. And we were wearing the UNRRA uniforms, which were similar to German uniforms. But they were kind of khaki green, you know, of course, without any insignias. But they were-- but they looked-- and we spoke perfect German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got mixed up with this group of Germans. And they had professional guides who were trained to smuggle people across the border. And we, you know, we asked to be smuggled across as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they, they-- we smuggled the whole night through. They had scouts they sent out to find out which point was OK to cross. And we crawled on our-- on our stomachs through the bushes. And finally, they got us on the other side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then they passed the hat for a collection. So the poor German soldiers had barely a few pfennigs. But we had those German marks. So we gave them. And they thanked us. And they thanked us.

speaker: interviewee
text: We told them that we were-- the reason they tolerated us was because they could tell who was native German and who isn't. But we told them that we were Volksdeutshe, which are Polish people of German origin. That's why we had that accent. Because they can tell who is German and who isn't. We told them we were Volksdeutshe, that we were escaping, that we want to go on the American side. And that's how we got across. And then we walked in-- part of the way, we walked. And we hitchhiked from Weimar until we got to Stuttgart. Because Stuttgart was a major UNRRA camp. And, well, there were a lot of people for Radom in Stuttgart. And one of the people that I was with was from Radom. And he heard there was a lot of people from Radom, so we went to Stuttgart. And it was a lot better in Stuttgart because it was an UNRRA camp. There was plenty to eat, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was a DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Displaced Person camp, yes. I'm sorry, I forgot to tell you. It was a Displaced Person camp run by the-- by UNRRA and the American UNRRA officers, you know, who worked in UNRRA. And while we were there in the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I asked to-- you know, everybody had to do something, you know. You couldn't just sit around.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had an opening in the camp militia. So I asked for a job as a camp policeman. Of course, we were not armed or anything, you know-- just stand and watch the lines where people standing in line for food or supplies.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then one day I go-- there was a restaurant on a corner. The name of the street was Reinsburgstrasse. Whoever was in Stuttgart will know that immediately. And there was a-- like, a Bier Stube on the corner, which is like a beer, beer hall and-- with food and everything. They had delicious German food, German soups, and everything. Of course, we went there a lot. And one day, I walked in there. And there's my wife, Johanna, again. So that was like a sign from heaven. Here is somebody I saw a thousand miles away. All of a sudden, I see her here, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's when I fell in love with her, you know, second time around. And she used to sing and dance. And, and life-- actually, that year that I was in the displaced camp was probably one of the best years of my life. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because we were free. We were young. We were dating, which I didn't have much time to do before because of the war. We were dating. We were dancing. We were singing. And we were carefree, you know. There was always food and decent clothing, you know, halfway decent anyway. So, in other words, it was like about the only time in my life that I look back [LAUGHS] that I had a carefree year. And then we got married there. I don't know if that's where you want me to break off or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anything ever made of the fact that you look German and you speak German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but many people, you see, coming back to Poland-- coming back to Warsaw, a lot of people asked me, why don't you go on the Polish side-- on the Aryan side? They called it the Aryan side. Now, the reason for that was, frankly, is that Polish people could tell who was Jewish. I don't know by what magic. And I used to speak Polish perfectly. I was at the top of my class in, in the Polish, Polish language, essays and everything. But they could tell who was Jewish. And if they saw a Jew, they would point out right away to the Germans-- Jude, Jude, Jude, you know. Sometimes they got something for it. Sometimes-- just-- they just didn't like Jews. They, they wanted to point the Jews out to the Germans. And when you did get caught on the Polish side, you usually got shot. You know, they just took you out and shot you. So it was a question of risking your life and being shot or going along with the flow. And frankly, I chose to go along with the flow. I know some people that went on Polish papers-- Polish side. But one reason also was-- why I couldn't intermingle with the Polish population is because we had no Polish sponsors. You had to have somebody who would be willing to, to protect you, to save you. Now my mother, who looked 100% Aryan, you know, not Jewish-- you saw her pictures-- she had many clients-- Polish clients who were well-off Polish people. They lived in estates with a fence and a gate around. And she went-- when the, the Germans started closing-- you know, tightening up the ghetto, she went begging to them that they should protect us. And they wouldn't do it, you know. As a matter of fact, in one place, they sent the dogs after her. And she got beat up-- bit, bit by the dogs something terrible. You know, we had to put compresses all over her arms and legs. Because that woman had a few bulldogs. And she sent them after my mother, although she pretended always to be a great friend and a, you know, a good customer. And she loved-- that she liked my mother and everything. But when it came to the crunch, it turned out what they really were, you know. And one reason the Polacks were, were interested in getting rid of the Jews is because when the Jew was sent out to concentration camp, they could come in and take over all their, their belongings, all their valuables. They had a free hand. And so that was one reason why, why this happened. As far as me being-- looking-- not looking Jewish, maybe it did help me in concentration camp. Maybe if I looked really what the caricatures, you know, describe-- depict as Jewish, with long noses, and so far, maybe life would be even harder on me. I don't know. I cannot-- I cannot say that. But I probably suffered as much as anybody, you know, regardless of my looks. But I also remember when they first grabbed us, when they were taking us through the Warsaw Ghetto to the Umschlagplatz, which was where they were gathering the Jews to send them to Majdanek and to Treblinka. As they were marching us, there were Polacks standing around and calling out all kinds of derogatory expressions like [POLISH]. You know, it's good that they're taking you away. Even little-- as I remember, it comes to me now, children in Taylor Tots-- in, you know, in walkers, who were maybe two or three years old, already-- were already calling [POLISH]. They couldn't say [POLISH]. They would say [POLISH], you know, like children cannot pronounced the R. But even the little kids were indoctrinated in this anti-Semitism, unfortunately, you know. So this was sad. Because I think that maybe if the Polish people would not be so happy to cooperate with the Nazis, if they would at least close their eyes, many, many more Jews would be left alive, you know. Even-- don't even talk about hiding out. But just close your eyes, pretend you don't see them, you know. So they could live their, their own life, you know. But unfortunately, it didn't happen. And the, the Polish police was very anti-Semitic. I'll never, never forget, in the ghetto, I saw-- an old lady was selling-- had a basket of bagels to sell. And I guess they had to pay off the, the Polish police. And she maybe didn't pay off because he dumped her whole basket of bagels into the ground. And he stomped on it, you know, crunched them all up, you know. This poor old lady, you know, that's probably all she had in the world. So that's coming back and answering your question.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you as a Jew, a survivor of a concentration camp, living in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was good and bad. Of course, it was good that we were free. We could go wherever we want to. We could take the street cart and go. We could-- we played soccer. We played volleyball and basketball on the-- on the playgrounds.

speaker: interviewee
text: But also what bothered us a lot is that the Germans were living wonderful. They still had their beautiful homes, villas. I used to walk around and envy them, you know? Here they has a beautiful villa with a fence, with a gate, and dogs and everything. So really, the, the war, in many instances, didn't affect them that much, I suppose with the exception of the ones that got bombed out, you know. But they lived a very good life. And this is-- this bothered us a lot. And also, they had the German police, who also were armed, you know, walking around, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, like I said, I wasn't in Germany that long. We-- I wasn't interested in doing any business. I was just interested in leaving as soon as possible. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I proposed, you see, what happened is that-- I remember I had in my mind one address that I remember when my brother left in 1938. It was an older cousin. It was my wife-- my mother's first cousin. She was my second cousin. But she was an older lady. And she wanted to take us both out of Poland into-- to America. But my mother couldn't stand the thought of losing both sons at the same time. So she allowed my older brother to leave-- to leave. And she insisted that I should stay. Of course, she meant well. It didn't work out the way, unfortunately. But I remember that one address, which was in Denver. They lived in Denver, Colorado. So after the war, I wrote a letter. I also remembered an, an address in Israel from my grandmother and my grandfather, my aunts that I had there that left in the '30s.
